questions.q: Let $G=\Gal(\mathbb{Q}(i)/\mathbb{Q})=\langle\sigma\rangle$. Which statement is Hilbert's Theorem 90 in this case?
choices: $H^1(G,\mathbb{Q}(i)^\times)=1$, so every norm-one $\beta\in\mathbb{Q}(i)^\times$ has the form $\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha$.
Every element of $\mathbb{Q}(i)$ is a sum of two squares.
questions.hint: Hilbert 90 is about cyclic Galois extensions and norm-one elements.
questions.explain: Hilbert 90 says $H^1$ of a cyclic Galois group with coefficients in $L^\times$ vanishes. The cocycle attached to $\beta\in\ker N$ is then a coboundary $g\mapsto\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha$.

questions.q: A 1-cochain $\varphi:G\to M$ is a cocycle iff
questions.hint: The action of $g$ on the second factor twists the homomorphism law.
questions.explain: The 1-cocycle (crossed homomorphism) condition is $\varphi(gh)=\varphi(g)+g\cdot\varphi(h)$. When the action is trivial it collapses to an ordinary homomorphism.

questions.hint: $H^0$ is fixed points; a nonzero element of $\bar K$ fixed by every automorphism lies in $K$.
questions.explain: By definition $H^0(G,M)=M^G$. The fixed field of $\Gal(\bar K/K)$ is $K$, so the fixed elements of $\bar K^\times$ are $K^\times$.

questions.q: A $G$-torsor under $A$ is a $G$-set $X$ on which $A$ acts simply transitively, with the $G$-action and $A$-action compatible. What is the role of $H^1(G,A)$?
choices: It is the pointed set of isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors under $A$.
It computes the Galois group of $X$.
It is always trivial when $A$ is abelian.
questions.explain: $H^1(G,A)$ parameterises isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors under $A$; the trivial class is the trivial torsor $A$ acting on itself.

questions.q: By Galois descent, $H^1(\Gal(\bar K/K),\mathrm{PGL}_n(\bar K))$ classifies
choices: Central simple algebras over $K$ of degree $n$.
questions.explain: $\mathrm{PGL}_n(\bar K)=\mathrm{Aut}(M_n(\bar K))$ as a $\bar K$-algebra, so its $H^1$ classifies $\bar K/K$-twisted forms of $M_n(K)$ — exactly the central simple $K$-algebras of degree $n$.

choices: The only $\bar K/K$-twisted form of $K^n$ as a $K$-vector space is itself — Hilbert 90 generalised.
The action factors through a finite quotient.
questions.hint: All $K$-vector spaces of the same dimension are isomorphic.
questions.explain: There is only one $K$-vector space of dimension $n$ up to isomorphism, so there is only one $\bar K/K$-twisted form of $K^n$ — i.e. $H^1$ vanishes. This is the matrix-valued Hilbert 90.

questions.q: An extension $1\to A\to E\to G\to 1$ with $A$ abelian and central is split iff
questions.hint: A splitting is a section of $E\to G$ that is a homomorphism; the obstruction is the cocycle.
questions.explain: Split central extensions are exactly the trivial cocycle classes in $H^2(G,A)$. A non-zero class records an obstruction to choosing a homomorphic section.

questions.q: The Schur multiplier of a finite group $G$ is
choices: The number of conjugacy classes of $G$.
questions.hint: It controls the projective representations of $G$.
questions.explain: $M(G)=H^2(G,\mathbb{C}^\times)$ classifies central extensions by $\mathbb{C}^\times$. Every projective representation of $G$ lifts to a linear representation of a central extension by an element of $M(G)$.

questions.q: Up to equivalence, how many extensions $1\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to E\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 1$ exist (trivial action)?
choices: Two: the split extension $E=V_4$ and the cyclic $E=C_4$.
questions.explain: $H^2(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)=\mathbb{Z}/2$. The zero class gives $V_4=\mathbb{Z}/2\times\mathbb{Z}/2$; the nontrivial class gives $\mathbb{Z}/4$.

questions.q: Which is the Brauer group $\mathrm{Br}(K)$ as a Galois cohomology group?
questions.explain: By definition $\mathrm{Br}(K)=H^2(G_K,\bar K^\times)$. $H^1$ vanishes by Hilbert 90; $H^2$ first encounters interesting twisting and matches central simple algebras.

choices: $\mathbb{Z}/2$, generated by the class of Hamilton's quaternions $\mathbb{H}$.
questions.hint: Frobenius classified finite-dimensional division algebras over $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.explain: Over $\mathbb{R}$ the only finite-dimensional central division algebras are $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{H}$ (quaternions), giving $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$.

questions.q: The cyclic algebra $(a,b)_n$ is the $K$-algebra generated by $x,y$ with $x^n=a$, $y^n=b$, $yx=\zeta_n xy$. Which is true?
choices: Its class in $\mathrm{Br}(K)$ equals the cup product of $a,b\in K^\times/(K^\times)^n\cong H^1(K,\mu_n)$.
It is always a matrix algebra.
questions.explain: By Kummer theory $K^\times/(K^\times)^n=H^1(K,\mu_n)$ when $\mu_n\subset K$. The cup product lands in $H^2(K,\mu_n\otimes\mu_n)\cong H^2(K,\mu_n)\subset\mathrm{Br}(K)[n]$ and is represented by the cyclic algebra $(a,b)_n$.

gcb-cup-product-cassels-tate.title: Cup product and Tate local duality

questions.q: Local Tate duality for a non-archimedean local field $K$ with finite Galois module $M$ gives a perfect pairing
questions.hint: The dualizing module is $\bar K^\times$, whose $H^2$ is $\mathrm{Br}(K)=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ for local $K$.
questions.explain: For a local field the Brauer group is canonically $\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$, and local Tate duality pairs $H^i(K,M)$ with $H^{2-i}(K,\Hom(M,\bar K^\times))$ into it.

questions.q: The cup product on Galois cohomology
questions.hint: Cocycle-level commutativity holds only up to a coboundary; sign appears on cohomology.
questions.explain: The Alexander–Whitney cup product satisfies graded-commutativity on cohomology classes but only up to coboundaries on cochains.

questions.q: Local class field theory says that for a non-archimedean local $K$, the local invariant
questions.hint: Each finite cyclic unramified extension contributes $\mathbb{Z}/n$.
questions.explain: For non-archimedean local $K$, the unramified Brauer group is $\mathrm{Br}(K^{\mathrm{ur}}/K)=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ via Frobenius, and this exhausts $\mathrm{Br}(K)$.

questions.q: Albert–Brauer–Hasse–Noether says: a central simple algebra $A$ over a number field $K$ is split iff
choices: It is split over every completion $K_v$ of $K$.
questions.explain: ABHN gives the exact sequence $0\to\mathrm{Br}(K)\to\bigoplus_v\mathrm{Br}(K_v)\xrightarrow{\sum\mathrm{inv}_v}\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}\to 0$, so vanishing locally everywhere implies vanishing globally.

choices: An obstruction to local-everywhere points lifting to a global rational point, computed by pairing with Brauer classes on $X$.
A bound on the rank of an elliptic curve.
The discriminant of a Galois extension.
questions.hint: It refines the Hasse principle when the principle fails.
questions.explain: For a smooth proper variety $X$, points of $X(\mathbb{A}_K)$ orthogonal to every Brauer class on $X$ form $X(\mathbb{A}_K)^{\mathrm{Br}}\supseteq X(K)$; failure of equality is the Brauer–Manin obstruction.

questions.q: In BSD-style descent on an elliptic curve $E/K$, the Selmer group $\mathrm{Sel}_n(E/K)$ sits in an exact sequence
questions.hint: Selmer is the local-global Galois cohomology kernel; Tate–Shafarevich is what's left after subtracting the global Mordell–Weil image.
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Sel}_n$ is the kernel of $H^1(K,E[n])\to\prod_v H^1(K_v,E)$. Its image of the Kummer map is $E(K)/nE(K)$; the cokernel is $\mathrm{III}(E/K)[n]$.
